retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Risk Assessment -- Contraindications -- Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting
retrieved_docs.content: Similar to coronary angiography, patients should not prophylactically undergo coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG), as there is no increase in perioperative outcomes. [10] The ACC/AHA guideline recommendations are that patients who are revascularized by CABG within the past 5 years can proceed with noncardiac surgery without further intervention. [10] According to the Bypass Angioplasty Revascularization Investigation (BARI) trial, the determination is that these patients, as well as those who have undergone PCI, have a lower risk of developing postoperative cardiac complications when compared to those who have not been revascularized.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Stunning and Hibernation -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Primary prevention of myocardial stunning and hibernation focuses on managing risk factors for CAD, such as hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and smoking, through lifestyle modifications and medications such as statins, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, and β-blockers. In secondary prevention, early detection using imaging techniques such as echocardiography or nuclear stress testing facilitates timely interventions, including revascularization procedures such as PCI or CABG.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Redo -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications of repeat revascularisation. Review the challenges anticipated with redo sternotomy in general and redo CABG in particular. Describe the surgical approach and myocardial protection strategy in redo CABG as well as conduit selection. Summarize the importance of secondary prevention measures. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Atrial fibrillation within the first 5 days after CABG is relatively common with rates of 20% to 50% and is associated with increased morbidity with a higher risk of embolic stroke postoperatively as well as increased mortality. Preoperative treatment with beta-blockers and possibly amiodarone has been shown to be the most effective way to reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation. [2] The risk of perioperative death after CABG is dependent on co-morbidities, the urgency of the surgery, and the case-volume of the center where the operation takes place, ranging from 1% to 2%. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Medical therapy requires optimization in the perioperative period according to ACC/AHA consensus guidelines, summarized here. Provided the patient has an ejection fraction (EF) >30%, beta-blockers should be dosed for at least 24 hours before CABG to prevent mortality and atrial fibrillation. This benefit extends into the post-hospitalization period for some patients. If there are contraindications to beta-blockers, amiodarone may be a substitute as prophylaxis for atrial fibrillation. Digoxin may be useful as rate control for postoperative atrial fibrillation, but not as prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Stroke remains a particular Achilles heel of CABG surgery, with an incidence of 1.4% to 3.8%, in addition to adding a ten-fold increase in mortality. Etiologies include embolization from aortic cannulation, microemboli from cardiopulmonary bypass, and the potential for low flow hypoperfusion of borderline ischemic brains. Prevention strategies include optimal medical therapy, carotid endarterectomy for patients meeting indications, and operative adjuncts such as epiaortic ultrasound and transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) to guide aortic cannulation. [13]

retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Revascularization [3] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Indications -- Class I indications for CABG
retrieved_docs.content: Stenosis of three vessels >70%

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Indications -- Class I indications for CABG
retrieved_docs.content: Stenosis of the left main >50% without a patent bypass graft (unprotected left main disease)

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative medications such as beta-blockers are most often continued throughout the perioperative period to prevent arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation. Aspirin was previously withheld 5 to 7 days before surgery, but recently, it has been recommended to be started or continued preoperatively.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the anatomical structures, indications, and contraindications of coronary artery bypass grafting. Describe the equipment, personnel, preparation, and technique in regards to coronary artery bypass grafting. Outline the potential complications of coronary artery bypass grafting. Review interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance coronary artery bypass grafting and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications to CABG include patient refusal, coronary arteries incompatible with grafting, and the absence of viable myocardium to graft.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Redo -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Redo CABG is usually contraindicated in cases where severe established myocardial infarction has taken place with extensive areas affected and no evidence of myocardial viability present. Patients with multiple comorbidities and low life expectancy normally will not benefit from the long term effect on survival, and discussion among the team and with the patients usually takes place to decide the best medical management.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Surgery -- Indications -- Class I indications for CABG
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency CABG for failed PCI if substantial myocardium or hemodynamic stability is at risk

retrieved_docs.title: Aortofemoral Bypass -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: In preparation for the procedure, you should ask your patient to partake in [16] : Tobacco cessation at least 3 to 4 weeks before surgery Exercising In addition to specific patient preparation, the surgeon should also have on hand [17] [18] : Blood on call to the operating room for the patient, should it be needed, (with cell saver available) Addition, pre-operative stress testing, cardiac angiography, or intervention (open or percutaneously) have reduced mortality for aortic operations. [19] Perioperative antibiotics should be administered to help decrease surgical site wound infections. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Internal Mammary Artery Bypass -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is a complicated surgical procedure that requires operative skills and expertise as well as good perioperative care. Therefore it is necessary to have a system to ensure the completion of all aspects of patient care. In pre-operative care, in addition to discussing the risks and benefits of the procedure with the patient and family, the assessment must be done to ensure that patient meets the recommended criteria for the procedure. Proper sterile technique is fundamental to better outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2822
retrieved_docs.content: Meloxikam is contraindicated in patients undergoing coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery, meaning that the medication should be avoided in the peri‑operative setting of this cardiac revascularization procedure due to the risk of adverse effects or harmful interactions. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe medication management and optimal outcomes in individuals receiving CABG.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is a specialized therapeutic procedure for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب). However, CABG is contraindicated in the presence of the disease ketorolak, indicating that patients diagnosed with ketorolak should not undergo this surgical intervention. This knowledge helps clinicians select appropriate candidates for CABG and avoid performing the procedure when ketorolak is present, thereby improving patient safety and treatment outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is a specialized therapeutic procedure for heart disease, classified under the Arabic term الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب, which denotes a cardiac‑specific intervention. The medication meloxikam is contraindicated in the context of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), indicating that meloxikam should not be administered to patients who are undergoing or have undergone this surgical procedure because of potential adverse effects. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe peri‑operative management and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is classified as a specific therapeutic procedure for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب) and falls within the broader group of cardiac therapeutic interventions, which also includes the subcategory of unclassified cardiac procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بأمراض القلب، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Clinical guidance indicates that CABG is contraindicated in the presence of the conditions ketorolak and meloxikam, meaning that patients diagnosed with these diseases should not undergo this surgical revascularization. Recognizing the hierarchical placement of CABG within cardiac treatment modalities and its contraindications helps clinicians select appropriate therapeutic strategies and avoid procedures that may be harmful in the context of ketorolak or meloxikam.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is contraindicated in the presence of the agents ketorolak and meloxikam, indicating that patients undergoing CABG should not be treated with either ketorolak or meloxikam because these medications may pose a risk of adverse outcomes when combined with the surgical procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic procedures for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب) include a range of interventions, among which coronary artery bypass graft (cabg) is a specific type. Coronary artery bypass graft (cabg) is classified as an instance of the cardiac therapeutic procedure and is also connected to the broader category of cardiopulmonary bypass (المجازة القلبية الرئوية) through the subcategory of unclassified cardiac therapeutic procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بأمراض القلب، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Clinically, coronary artery bypass graft (cabg) is contraindicated in patients with the conditions ketorolak and meloxikam, indicating that these diseases must be assessed before the procedure is considered. Recognizing the hierarchical relationships among cardiac therapeutic procedures and the specific contraindications for coronary artery bypass graft (cabg) supports clinicians in making informed treatment decisions and minimizing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery is contraindicated with the use of meloxikam and trometamolová sůl ketorolaku; both agents should be avoided in patients undergoing CABG because their administration can increase the risk of peri‑operative complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 554
retrieved_docs.content: diagnostics, cardiac surgical interventions, ultrasound‑guided and other minimally invasive therapies, and exercise‑based rehabilitative modalities, emphasizing an integrated approach to diagnosis, treatment, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2822
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is classified as a specific therapeutic procedure for heart diseases (الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب) and therefore falls within the broader group of cardiac therapeutic interventions, which also includes the subcategory of unclassified heart‑disease procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بأمراض القلب، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). In the context of patient care, the use of meloxikam (meloxicam) is contraindicated for patients undergoing CABG, indicating that this non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug should be avoided before, during, and after the surgical procedure to prevent adverse outcomes. This hierarchical and safety information guides clinicians in both selecting appropriate cardiac procedures and managing medication regimens around CABG.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2118
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) is classified as a specific “الإجراء العلاجي الخاص بأمراض القلب” (therapeutic procedure for heart disease). Within this category, the broader group “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بأمراض القلب، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (therapeutic procedures for heart disease, not classified elsewhere) is a child of the same parent category and is linked to “المجازة القلبية الرئوية” (cardiopulmonary bypass) as its parent procedure. This hierarchical relationship indicates that both CABG and the uncategorized therapeutic procedures are subsets of general cardiac therapeutic interventions, with cardiopulmonary bypass serving as an overarching technique that underlies these procedures. Additionally, the use of CABG is contraindicated in the presence of ketorolac (referred to as “ketorolak”), reflecting the clinical need to avoid this non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug due to its potential to increase bleeding risk during cardiac surgery. Understanding these connections helps clinicians organize cardiac surgical options, recognize the role of cardiopulmonary bypass, and avoid contraindicated medication use to improve patient safety.
